summary: Gruber examines why Apple continues to enforce the iPhone SDK NDA even after the SDK has shipped, frustrating developers who want to openly collaborate. He highlights a reader's theory that the NDA exists to protect Apple's patent strategy around multi-touch technology. Under patent law, keeping the SDK under NDA means its inventions remain 'unpublished,' preserving Apple's ability to file patents—especially important in the EU where publication can forfeit patent claims. Gruber finds this the most credible explanation he's encountered for Apple's otherwise baffling behavior.
key_points: The iPhone SDK NDA blocks the open collaboration (mailing lists, blogs, reference books) that Cocoa developers depend on
A reader proposes that the NDA preserves Apple's patent filing options by keeping SDK inventions legally 'unpublished'
EU patent law is stricter than US law—publication can forfeit patent claims entirely, giving Apple strong incentive to maintain the NDA
The breadth or leakiness of the NDA doesn't matter legally; what matters is that the USPTO treats everything under NDA as unpublished
money_quotes: Cocoa developers are used to having public mailing lists, weblog posts, and third-party reference books at their disposal. The NDA forbids this sort of openness.
But the other thing is it seems baffling; it's hard to see how Apple benefits by not lifting the NDA.

It doesn't matter at all how broad/leaky the NDA process is, in the eyes of the USPTO, every invention in the iPhone SDK is a non-published invention and will continue to be so until the NDA is lifted.
So as if the entire field of software patents weren't already enough of a drag on the industry, we may well have it to blame for the NDA.
key_insight: Apple's seemingly irrational continuation of the iPhone SDK NDA likely serves a patent strategy: keeping the SDK legally 'unpublished' preserves their ability to file multi-touch patents, particularly under stricter EU patent rules.
